frames.description: the ground truth — what actually happened at the Lantern House
Wend Aldercott, deputy ford-warden, the POV — what Wend learns night by night
Halloran Voss, the circuit magistrate's clerk — what he knows and believes
Berdine Quist, the wool-factor — what he knows, and what he wants believed
Tomas Reke, the discharged soldier — what he knows and believes
Cray Holt, the hooded traveller — what he knows and conceals
Senna Cobb, the midwife — what she saw and withheld
Old Pell, the tinker who knows the warden — what he knows and believes
Garrec, the horse-trader — what he knows and believes
Lyle, the drover travelling with Garrec — what he knows and believes
Aldous Frith, the young man of the eloping couple — what he knows and believes
Joan Tarry, the young woman of the eloping couple — what she knows and believes
Mabon Aldercott, the old ford-warden (Wend's uncle), absent then dead — what he knew before he died

branches.description: NAME — Wend lays the matter before the circuit clerk and lets the law take it
HOLD — Wend keeps what he learned quiet and lets the travellers cross unhindered
ACT — Wend settles the matter at the Lantern House himself before anyone crosses

entities.description: Wend Aldercott, deputy ford-warden, left in charge
Mabon Aldercott, the old ford-warden, Wend's uncle
Cray Holt, the hooded traveller; Mabon's creditor
the deed-bond pledging the Lantern House against Mabon's debt
the chain-ferry log book kept by the warden
the clerk's sealed assize papers, bearing the county seal
the wet hooded cloak the midwife found drying
the river lantern lit at the ford each dusk

predicates.description: who is responsible for Mabon's death
the contents-state of the strongbox at arrival
whether the deed-bond is genuine or forged

facts.claim: Mabon did not go downriver on warden's business; he went to meet his creditor Cray Holt the day before the flood
Mabon had pledged the Lantern House itself against a gambling debt to Cray, sealed in a deed-bond
Cray's deed-bond is a forgery — Mabon's real debt was smaller and already half-paid; Cray forged the pledge of the whole house
the morning the house filled, Mabon was still alive — he had crossed by the chain-ferry to meet Cray and meant to return by dark
on the first flood night Mabon met Cray at the millrace to refuse the forged bond; they struggled and Mabon drowned
Cray is responsible for Mabon's death at the millrace
the wool-factor's strongbox was already nearly empty when Berdine arrived — a thin layer of coin over lead weights
Berdine had embezzled his master's coin before the journey; the near-empty box is the evidence he must hide
Berdine broke his own strongbox open in the night to stage a robbery and explain the missing coin
the cry and crash in the night were Berdine's staged break-in; the splash that followed was Mabon going into the millrace — two separate events the house heard as one
after the struggle Cray took the deed-bond from Mabon's body and kept it on him under the hooded cloak
Cray's cloak was soaked at the millrace; he hung it to dry where the midwife later found it
Mabon tore the last leaves from the chain-ferry log so no record would show he had taken the ferry across to meet Cray
the clerk's sealed papers carry the genuine county seal, the same seal a forger would have to match to fake a deed-bond
Wend, deputy, lights the river lantern alone as the snowmelt rises and the ford begins to flood
Wend takes in twelve stranded travellers as the water climbs over the crossing
Wend finds his uncle Mabon's cell empty and is told only that the warden went downriver on business
Wend sees the wool-factor carry a heavy iron strongbox to his cell and bolt the door
Wend notes the clerk's papers bear a pressed county seal when the clerk lays them on the board
Wend wakes to a cry and a crash in the night and then the river's roar, and cannot tell what he heard
in the morning Wend finds the strongbox broken open and its coin gone
Wend hears Berdine accuse the hooded traveller of the theft and demand he be searched
the tinker tells Wend on the second night that Mabon had fallen into debt to a hard man over the winter
Wend finds the last leaves of the chain-ferry log torn out and knows Mabon kept that log faithfully for thirty years
Wend learns of the deed-bond pledging the Lantern House and understands the ford itself was the stake
Wend and the soldier pull Mabon's body from the millrace and Wend knows his uncle is dead, not downriver

confronting the hooded traveller, Wend learns the man is Cray Holt, Mabon's creditor, that the deed-bond is on him, and — against the tinker's word that the debt was small and half-paid — sees the whole-house pledge overreaches and must be forged
Berdine, cornered by Wend's questions about the box, lets slip that the coin was never there to steal
by the third dawn Wend believes Cray killed Mabon over the forged bond and Berdine staged his own robbery, and must choose his road as the water falls
the clerk believes the hooded traveller robbed the strongbox in the night, taking Berdine's accusation at face value
the clerk guards his sealed assize papers and will not let them be opened before the magistrate, but knows the county seal by heart
the clerk does not yet know the warden is dead; he thinks Mabon is downriver and will return to clear the ford
Berdine knows the strongbox held almost nothing — lead under a skim of coin — because he emptied it himself before the road
Berdine intends everyone to believe a thief robbed a full box, so the missing money is the thief's crime and not his
Berdine fixes on the nameless hooded traveller as the safest man to accuse, since no one will vouch for him
Berdine does not know the warden is dead or that any deed-bond exists; the warden's affairs are nothing to him
Cray came to Corvath to collect on the deed-bond and took the far cell, giving no name, to stay unknown
Cray knows the deed-bond is forged — he made the pledge of the whole house himself, beyond Mabon's true debt
Cray knows Mabon drowned in the struggle at the millrace and that he, Cray, left him in the race
Cray believes Berdine's robbery uproar is a gift — the house hunting a coin-thief looks nowhere near the millrace
Cray means to cross the moment the water falls and carry the deed-bond away to claim the Lantern House at law
the midwife, returning from a birth two valleys over, took the cell beside the hooded traveller's
in the grey morning the midwife found the hooded traveller's cloak hung sodden and reeking of river, hours after the rain had stopped
the midwife says nothing of the wet cloak at first, fearing a stranger's word against a stranger in a stranded house
the midwife tells Wend of the wet cloak only when the warden's body is found, knowing it now points at the hooded man
the tinker, who has crossed at Corvath a hundred times, knows Mabon well and says the warden would never leave the ford untended in flood season
the tinker knows Mabon had fallen to a hard man's debt over the winter, having seen the creditor's man dun him last autumn
the tinker tells Wend on the second night of the debt, the one fragment that turns Wend toward the deed-bond
the tinker does not connect the hooded traveller to the creditor; he never saw the dunning man's face
the soldier, walking home from a war he is not sure has ended, distrusts the watch the house keeps and takes the night watch himself
on his watch the soldier heard two sounds, not one — a near crash inside, then a far splash by the race — and quarrels with the drover who heard only the crash
the soldier helps Wend drag the body from the millrace and his memory of the far splash now fixes the time of the warden's death
the soldier believes the hooded traveller a deserter like men he knew in the war and is ready to think the worst of him
the horse-trader Garrec and the drover Lyle travel together but Garrec lets the house think Lyle merely hired on, to keep his own dealings quiet
the trader believes the robbery a plain coin-theft and cares only that the flood not cost him his string of horses
the drover Lyle heard only the single crash in the night and swears there was but one noise, contradicting the soldier
the drover knows Garrec is no mere master but his partner in the horse-dealing, and keeps that quiet as Garrec asks
the bridegroom Aldous crosses to be married against Joan's family's wishes and fears the flood will let her kin overtake them
the bridegroom believes nothing of the night but his own fear and held to his cell with Joan, hearing only muffled noise
the bride Joan, awake while Aldous slept, saw a hooded figure cross the yard toward the millrace in the dark
the bride says nothing of the hooded figure, afraid any notice will draw her family's pursuit down on the house
the bride tells Wend of the hooded figure crossing to the race only when the body is found, freeing her fear against her caution
Mabon knew the bond Cray held overreached his true debt and meant to refuse it to Cray's face at the millrace
Mabon tore the ferry log himself before crossing, meaning to leave no proof he had gone to meet a creditor he was ashamed of
Wend lays the whole matter before the clerk — the dead warden, the forged bond, the staged robbery — and asks the law to take it
the clerk, hearing Wend out, abandons his belief in a simple robbery and accepts that the warden was killed and the box was staged
the clerk opens his sealed papers and matches the genuine county seal against the deed-bond, proving the bond a forgery
Wend produces the wet-cloak account and the bride's hooded figure, and the clerk binds the deed-bond evidence to Cray
under the clerk's questioning Berdine's staged theft unravels: a robbed full box leaves coin-marks the empty box never had
NAME ending: the water falls and Wend gives Cray and Berdine over to the clerk for the assize; Wend keeps the ford his uncle kept
Wend tells no one what he learned, judging that a stranger's word and a torn log would hang the wrong or convict no one before the law
Wend lowers the chain-ferry and lets the house cross as the water falls, the young couple first, the soldier last
Cray crosses unhindered with the deed-bond still on him, free to press his forged claim on the Lantern House at law

alone after they cross, Wend burns the leaf of the deed-bond he copied from the body's papers, judging the whole-house pledge a forgery beyond his uncle's true debt and choosing silence over a doubtful charge
HOLD ending: the ford stands clear, Cray gone with the bond and his guilt, and Wend keeps the warden's chair alone with all he chose not to say
Wend raises the chain-ferry and shuts the ford against Cray before the water has fully fallen, settling the matter his own way
the soldier and the tinker stand with Wend at the lantern, the soldier's two-sound memory and the tinker's knowledge of the debt damning Cray to the house's face
cornered at the ford, Cray confesses to the warden's death and to forging the bond; Wend takes the deed-bond and burns it at the river lantern
Cray, breaking past Wend for the falling ford, is taken by the high water and drowns in the race where he left Mabon
ACT ending: the bond burned and Cray drowned, Wend lets the rest of the house cross knowing nothing, and keeps the ford and the secret both
